Low digital literacy and no internet access, combined with high authoritarian submission and low openness, lead to mild opposition to a mandate that seems complex, unnecessary, and potentially disruptive to social order.

High openness and very high extraversion, combined with low agreeableness and strong anti-domination values, produce mild support for giving users control over algorithms, but low digital literacy limits the strength of this stance.
Low openness to new experiences and moderate libertarian privacy concerns, combined with high agreeableness, lead to near-neutral ambivalence about mandating choice.
Low digital confidence and very low openness to new experiences make this person mildly skeptical of forcing algorithmic choice on users, as they prefer simple, top-down solutions.
High libertarianism emphasizing individual choice (anti-censorship 82.41%) and openness to experimentation strongly favor user control over algorithms.
Strong market deregulation views and anti-censorship orientation drive clear opposition to mandated user choice as unwarranted government intervention in algorithmic design.

peer_exemplars.reasoning: Very high trust in institutions and experts, combined with low openness and high loyalty, drive strong trust in mainstream journalism.
Very high trust in institutions and experts, combined with high agreeableness and low authoritarianism, strongly favor trust in mainstream journalism.

Extremely low trust in government and institutions, high anti-elitism, and a manichean worldview, combined with high collective narcissism, produce strong distrust of mainstream journalism as a tool of the corrupt elite.

Low agreeableness and low trust in others, combined with very high openness and low authoritarian submission, produce clear opposition to trusting mainstream journalism, as the person sees it as a tool of elite control.
Moderate trust in institutions and high system justification, tempered by some populist anti-elitism, result in mild trust in mainstream journalism.
Extremely high trust in all institutions, including science and the judiciary, extends to mainstream journalism, and very low openness to alternative views reinforces this strong trust.
Strong institutional trust (76.2%) provides baseline confidence, but high anti-elitism (73.6%) and manichean worldview (89.49%) introduce skepticism that caps absolute trust.

Despite high institutional trust in government and science, very low populist anti-elitism is inverted with low trust in media specifically, and the person's rural, low-digital-literacy background fosters skepticism of mainstream outlets.

Extremely high trust in government and institutions, combined with high agreeableness and low authoritarianism, leads to strong rejection of election distrust.

Very high trust in government and high system justification, combined with low dangerous-worldview and low authoritarian submission, produce mild opposition to distrusting elections, as the person generally trusts institutional processes.
